attributes.stem:
A 36-year-old woman presents to the emergency department after intentionally crashing her car, as an attempt to end her life. She is detained under the Mental Health Act and admitted to the inpatient psychiatric unit. She reports strong suicidal thoughts following an argument with her partner. Additionally, she describes that her episode of mental health decline started 2 months ago when she was involved in a bank heist while working as a bank teller, where her co-worker was shot and fatally injured. She reports experiencing intrusive memories and flashbacks to the situation and has since quit her job in an attempt to avoid these distressing feelings. Despite this, she describes not being able to recall the entire event. Furthermore, she has felt detached from others, particularly her family. Collateral history from her husband includes increased sleep disturbances and self-destructive behaviour in the last month. She has a history of borderline personality disorder and is accepting of this diagnosis, and is currently receiving dialectical behaviour therapy in the community, and engaging with a private psychiatrist. Her past psychiatric history involves 4 prior admissions for suicidal attempts after disputes with her partner in the past 5 years.

On mental state examination, she is irritable but cooperative. She denies ongoing suicidal ideation and is deemed at low acute risk of suicide.
attributes.stemQuestion: In addition to receiving DBT, which of the following would be the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Citalopram 20mg mane and review in 4 weeks
Discharge home with community mental health cognitive behavioural therapy
Trial of low dose atypical antipsychotics
attributes.explanation:
This patient's diagnosis is borderline personality disorder, and this is the primary cause of her repeated presentations with suicidal behaviour in situational crises. While there are now several effective, evidence-based treatments for borderline personality disorder the first one to be shown to be effective in trials and the one most commonly available in Australia is Dialectical Behaviour Therapy.

However, the other features she has shown are suggestive of post-traumatic stress disorder:

  • >1 month of symptoms following the traumatic event

  • Intrusion symptoms (recurrent intrusive memories and flashbacks)

  • Avoidance of stimuli (quitting work)

  • Negative effect on cognitions and mood (dissociative amnesia and detached from others)

  • Altered reactivity (sleep disturbance and self-destructive behaviour)

Exposure therapies such as Trauma-focused CBT and eye movement desensitisation and reprocessing are the first line treatment for post-traumatic stress disorder in adults. Exposure therapies have limited benefit when PTSD is delayed onset (i.e. >6 months after the traumatic incident). See Australian guidelines for evidence of different psychotherapies for PTSD.

Comorbid psychiatric conditions such as major depression and psychosis interfere with engagement with trauma-focused therapy. In these patients, it is useful to begin with pharmacological management and then review later (e.g. Citalopram 20mg mane and review in 4 weeks). Since this patient's post-traumatic stress disorder is not associated with Major depression or psychosis, this is not the correct option. However, in individuals with comorbid disorders (eg, depression, psychosis) that affect the person’s ability to work in trauma-focused therapy (eg, concentration, motivation), we treat with pharmacologic management until the individual’s symptoms are stable, then add psychotherapy.

Low-dose antipsychotics are not typically prescribed for PTSD. Antipsychotics have been suggested to benefit patients with hyper-arousal or re-experiencing symptom clusters. However, current research regarding the use of antipsychotics such as olanzapine, quetiapine, and aripiprazole demonstrates conflicting results, and limited evidence in improving symptoms associated with PTSD.

Maudsley-based therapy is a family-based therapy and is an accepted treatment for anorexia nervosa in adolescent individuals.

Cognitive behavioural therapy is an acceptable form of treatment for many psychiatric diagnoses; particularly major depressive disorder and anxiety disorders. Its efficacy in Borderline Personality Disorder is limited to treating discrete episodes of depression, anxiety and crisis and not the underlying disorder.
learningPoints: Dialectical behavioural therapy is the gold-standard treatment for a diagnosis of borderline personality disorder.
Prolonged exposure therapy is the first line treatment for post traumatic stress disorder with no significant comorbid psychiatric conditions (e.g. depression, psychosis)
references: Phoenix Australia. Australian guidelines for the prevention and treatment of acute stress disorder, posttraumatic stress disorder and complex PTSD. Melbourne: Phoenix Australia; 2020. https://www.phoenixaustralia.org/australian-guidelines-for-ptsd/
American Psychiatric Association. Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders. American Psychiatric Publishing; 2013: p. 947

Merz J, Schwarzer G, Gerger H. Comparative efficacy and acceptability of pharmacological, psychotherapeutic, and combination treatments in adults with posttraumatic stress disorder: a network meta-analysis. JAMA Psychiatry 2019;76(9):904–13. https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/31188399

Ahearn EP, Juergens T, Cordes T, Becker T, Krahn D. A review of atypical antipsychotic medications for posttraumatic stress disorder. Int Clin Psychopharmacol. 2011 Jul;26(4):193-200. doi: 10.1097/YIC.0b013e3283473738. PMID: 21597381.
